papers.title: Shared neural substrates of prosocial and parenting behaviours
papers.authors: Fangmiao Sun, Kayla Y. Lim, James Dang, Li I. Zhang, Ye Emily Wu et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: Researchers identified shared neural substrates in the brain that are associated with prosocial and parenting behaviors. This study suggests that these behaviors may be linked to specific neural mechanisms. The findings have implications for understanding the neural basis of social behavior and may inform the development of interventions for social disorders.

papers.title: Heart surgery with quick-setting magnetic fluid could prevent strokes
papers.authors: Nick Petrić Howe, Shamini Bundell
papers.summary: A quick-setting magnetic fluid has been developed that could potentially prevent strokes by filling the area where blood clots can form. Animal trials showed that the fluid was safe and effective. This technology has the potential to revolutionize stroke prevention.

papers.title: Magnetic gel injected into the heart could stop strokes
papers.authors: Nick Petrić Howe
papers.summary: A magnetic gel has been injected into the heart to block a part of the heart and reduce the risk of stroke. The gel was shown to be effective in animal trials. This technology has the potential to prevent strokes in humans.

papers.title: Genome modelling and design across all domains of life with Evo 2
papers.authors: Garyk Brixi, Matthew G. Durrant, Jerome Ku, Mohsen Naghipourfar, Michael Poli et al. (+57 more)
papers.summary: A new artificial intelligence-based model called Evo 2 has been developed that can predict functional properties from genomic sequences and provide a rich generative model for researchers in biology. Evo 2 was trained on 9 trillion DNA base pairs spanning all domains of life.

papers.title: DICER cleavage fidelity is governed by 5′-end binding pockets
papers.authors: Minh Khoa Ngo, Cong Truc Le, Tuan Anh Nguyen
papers.summary: Researchers used cryo-electron microscopy and massively parallel assays to study the mechanism by which DICER, a key enzyme in the RNase III family, cleaves RNA at precise locations to produce small RNAs. The study provides new insights into the mechanism of DICER.

papers.title: Limited thermal tolerance in tropical insects and its genomic signature
papers.authors: Kim L. Holzmann, Thomas Schmitzer, Antonia Abels, Marko Čorkalo, Oliver Mitesser et al. (+13 more)
papers.summary: A study of tropical insects found that species from lowland areas have low thermal tolerance and that this is due to the fundamental properties of protein architecture.

papers.title: A metabolic alarmin from keratinocytes potentiates systemic humoral immunity
papers.authors: Zhenglin Ji, Ji Gao, Shaocun Zhang, Jiajie Li, Haijing Wu et al. (+39 more)
papers.summary: A metabolic intermediate called farnesyl pyrophosphate acts as an alarmin to enhance the immune system's ability to produce antibodies.

papers.title: Long-term thrombus-free left atrial appendage occlusion via magnetofluids
papers.authors: Shu Wang, Wenhao Ju, Donglin Zhuang, Zhecheng Chen, Dongliang Zhao et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: Researchers developed a new method for occluding the left atrial appendage using magnetofluids, which can resist high-speed blood flow in the presence of a magnetic field.

papers.title: Lipid metabolism drives dietary effects on T cell ferroptosis and immunity
papers.authors: Naiqi Wang, Zhian Chen, Yin Yao, Chenglong Sun, Wei Wei et al. (+35 more)
papers.summary: The balance of fatty acids in the diet affects the susceptibility of T cells to ferroptosis, a type of cell death, and plays a key role in regulating T cell function.

papers.title: Merlin: a computed tomography vision–language foundation model and dataset
papers.authors: Louis Blankemeier, Ashwin Kumar, Joseph Paul Cohen, Jiaming Liu, Longchao Liu et al. (+35 more)
papers.summary: A new vision-language foundation model called Merlin was developed and trained on a large dataset of paired CT scans and patient record data, demonstrating strong performance across various tasks.

papers.title: Microbiota-mediated induction of beige adipocytes in response to dietary cues
papers.authors: Takeshi Tanoue, Manabu Nagayama, Ayumi J. A. Roochana, Samuel Zimmerman, Orr Ashenberg et al. (+28 more)
papers.summary: A low-protein diet in mice leads to a change in their fat tissue, with the help of gut bacteria, to burn energy like brown fat.

papers.title: Mechanism of co-transcriptional cap snatching by influenza polymerase
papers.authors: Alexander Helmut Rotsch, Delong Li, Maud Dupont, Tim Krischuns, Ute Neef et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered how the influenza virus takes a piece of the host's RNA to help it replicate, a key step in the virus's life cycle.

papers.title: Cell-free chromatin state tracing reveals disease origin and therapy responses
papers.authors: Xubin Chen, Xiaoxuan Meng, Weilong Zhang, Xiawei Zhang, Yaping Zhang et al. (+26 more)
papers.summary: Scientists developed a new method to analyze DNA in the blood to identify the source of diseases and track how well treatments are working.

papers.title: The oldest articulated bony fish from the early Silurian period
papers.authors: You-An Zhu, Yang Chen, Qiang Li, Wen-Jin Zhao, Zheng-Da Zhou et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Fossil discovery in China revealed the oldest known remains of a bony fish, which lived over 420 million years ago.

papers.title: Bulk hexagonal diamond
papers.authors: Shoulong Lai, Xigui Yang, Jiuyang Shi, Shijie Liu, Ying Guo et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Researchers successfully synthesized a new form of diamond, called hexagonal diamond, by compressing graphite under high pressure and temperature.

papers.title: Sea level much higher than assumed in most coastal hazard assessments
papers.authors: Katharina Seeger, Philip S. J. Minderhoud
papers.summary: A new study finds that the global mean sea level is higher than previously assumed in most coastal hazard assessments, which could impact coastal communities and ecosystems.

papers.title: A glucocorticoid–FAS axis controls immune evasion during metastatic seeding
papers.authors: Monica Cassandras, Xavier Sanchez, Lauren Hsu, Yu Huang, Adam J. Getzler et al. (+17 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have identified a key driver of resistance to cancer treatment in triple-negative breast cancer, which could lead to new therapeutic strategies.

papers.title: The molecular basis of force selectivity by PIEZO2
papers.authors: Eric M. Mulhall, Oleg Yarishkin, Rose Z. Hill, Anna K. Koster, Ardem Patapoutian
papers.summary: Scientists have discovered the molecular basis of force selectivity by PIEZO2, a channel that plays a crucial role in mechanosensation.

papers.title: Advancing operational global aerosol forecasting with machine learning
papers.authors: Ke Gui, Xutao Zhang, Huizheng Che, Lei Li, Yu Zheng et al. (+17 more)
papers.summary: A machine-learning-driven forecasting system has been developed to improve operational global aerosol forecasting, enabling more accurate predictions of aerosol optical components and surface concentrations.

papers.title: Wind shear enhances soil moisture influence on rapid thunderstorm growth
papers.authors: Christopher M. Taylor, Cornelia Klein, Emma J. Barton, Sebastian Hahn, Wolfgang Wagner
papers.summary: Researchers have found that wind shear amplifies the impact of soil moisture in triggering rapidly developing thunderstorms, which could improve our understanding of thunderstorm dynamics.

papers.title: Structural basis of RNA-guided transcription by a dCas12f–σ<sup>E</sup>–RNAP complex
papers.authors: Renjian Xiao, Florian T. Hoffmann, Dan Xie, Tanner Wiegand, Adriana I. Palmieri et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: A study reveals the structural basis of RNA-guided transcription by a dCas12f–σ<sup>E</sup>–RNAP complex, which initiates transcription at a fixed downstream distance without recognizing canonical promoter elements.

papers.title: Homologous recombination deficiency and hemizygosity drive resistance in breast cancer
papers.authors: Anton Safonov, Minna Lee, David N. Brown, Luca Boscolo Bielo, Miika Mehine et al. (+52 more)
papers.summary: Researchers identified actionable genomic patterns driving acquired therapy resistance in breast cancer, which are defined by germline and somatic interactions.

papers.title: Exapted CRISPR–Cas12f homologues drive RNA-guided transcription
papers.authors: Florian T. Hoffmann, Tanner Wiegand, Adriana I. Palmieri, Juniper Glass-Klaiber, Renjian Xiao et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: A study found that specialized σ factors interact with nuclease-dead, CRISPR–Cas12f proteins to form potent, RNA-guided gene activation systems that function independently of fixed promoter motifs.

papers.title: Wide-swath altimetry maps bank shapes and storage changes in global rivers
papers.authors: A. Cerbelaud, J. Wade, C. H. David, M. Durand, R. P. M. Frasson et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: A new study used satellite data to map river channel geometry and monthly water storage changes for 126,674 river reaches worldwide, providing insights into global river dynamics.

papers.title: Largest Silurian fish illuminates the origin of osteichthyan characters
papers.authors: Jing Lu, Brian Choo, Wenjin Zhao, You-an Zhu, Xindong Cui et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: A new fossil discovery of the largest Silurian fish, Megamastax amblyodus, provides previously unseen morphological details of a Silurian stem osteichthyan, shedding light on the origin of osteichthyan characters.

papers.title: Precancerous niche remodelling dictates nascent tumour persistence
papers.authors: G. Skrupskelyte, J. E. Rojo Arias, H. Ajith, Y. Dang, D. Rossetti et al. (+16 more)
papers.summary: A study found that a specific type of niche in the stomach can support the growth of tumors. This niche is created by fibroblasts that remodel the underlying tissue in response to tumor stress.

papers.title: Rising temperatures pose a threat to tropical insects
papers.authors: Vojtech Novotny
papers.summary: Rising temperatures may lead to a loss of insect biodiversity in tropical lowlands. An assessment of thousands of insect species found that they are sensitive to increasing temperatures.

papers.title: Fresh claim of making elusive ‘hexagonal’ diamond is the strongest yet
papers.authors: Mark Peplow
papers.summary: Researchers claim to have found evidence of a rare form of diamond called hexagonal diamond. This form of diamond has been debated for decades.

papers.title: Stress-hormone signalling protects spreading cancer cells from immune system
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Cancer cells can evade the immune system by using stress-hormone signalling. This signalling pathway may be a potential target for preventing metastasis.

papers.title: AI can write genomes — how long until it creates synthetic life?
papers.authors: Ewen Callaway
papers.summary: A computer model can generate short genome sequences, but it is not yet capable of writing genomes that can be used in living cells. Further advances are needed to create synthetic life.

papers.title: Magnetic fluid offers better seal in heart-plugging medical procedure
papers.authors: Yu Shrike Zhang
papers.summary: An injectable fluid has been used to close off part of the heart in animals, potentially improving a procedure that prevents stroke in people with irregular heartbeats.

papers.title: Funding from individual donors: lessons from the Epstein case
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Universities need to establish and empower compliance teams to ensure adherence to ethical funding policies, particularly in light of the Epstein case.

papers.title: A genetic switch turns off parental behaviour and drives infanticide in male striped mice
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Research on African striped mice reveals that male parenting behavior is influenced by the social environment and expression level of the Agouti gene.

papers.title: Do wet or dry soils trigger thunderstorms? It depends on how the wind blows
papers.authors: Craig R. Ferguson
papers.summary: Wind conditions affect the connection between soil moisture and thunderstorms, according to satellite data, which could be used to inform forecasting.

papers.title: Climate change and geopolitics threaten water supplies — but disaster is not inevitable
papers.authors: Ali Tauqeer Sheikh
papers.summary: The Indus Waters Treaty has withstood several challenges, including armed conflicts and glacier loss, and could serve as a blueprint for other water management agreements.

papers.title: Cancer blood tests are everywhere. Do they really work?
papers.authors: Nic Fleming
papers.summary: Researchers question the effectiveness of cancer blood tests, which claim to detect multiple cancer types, but may be missing many cancers or delivering incorrect diagnoses.

papers.title: Skin cells boost distant antibody responses
papers.authors: Xueping Zhu, Caroline L. Sokol
papers.summary: Scientists have discovered a pathway in skin cells that boosts the production of antibodies to protect the whole body, providing new insights into immune defenses.

papers.title: How a mathematician is cracking open Mexico’s powerful drug cartels
papers.authors: Gemma Conroy
papers.summary: A mathematician has developed models of organized crime that could improve public safety in Mexico by providing a better understanding of the country's powerful drug cartels.

papers.title: Under pressure: the reality of Mexico’s research system
papers.authors: Oscar Xavier Guerrero Gutiérrez
papers.summary: Mexican scientists face unstable conditions, including lack of funding and resources, which can hinder their research and impact the country's scientific progress.

papers.title: Radiology AI makes consistent diagnoses using 3D images from different health centres
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A 3D vision-language model called Merlin has been trained to make consistent diagnoses using 3D images from different health centers, demonstrating its potential for broader clinical adoption.

papers.title: Hunt for reactive metabolites uncovers unusual chemistry in a human pathogen
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A new workflow was developed to capture reactive compounds produced by bacteria, leading to the discovery of two unusual molecules that could have broad synthetic applications.

papers.title: Author Correction: Healthy forests safeguard traditional wild meat food systems in Amazonia
papers.authors: André Pinassi Antunes, Pedro de Araujo Lima Constantino, Julia E. Fa, Daniel P. Munari, Thais Q. Morcatty et al. (+53 more)
papers.summary: A correction was made to a previous study on the importance of healthy forests in safeguarding traditional wild meat food systems in Amazonia.

papers.title: Genetically encoded assembly recorder temporally resolves cellular history
papers.authors: Yuqing Yan, Jiaxi Lu, Zhe Li, Zuohan Zhao, Timothy F. Shay et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: A genetically encoded assembly recorder was developed to temporally resolve cellular history, allowing for a deeper understanding of cellular processes.

papers.title: Jam-packed star system is most compact of its kind ever found
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A star system was discovered that is the most compact of its kind, with all four Sun-like stars fitting within the area of Jupiter's orbit.

papers.title: Releasing open-weight AI in steps would alleviate risks
papers.authors: S. M. Nuruzzaman Nobel, Maxine Tan
papers.summary: Releasing open-weight AI in steps could alleviate risks associated with its development and deployment.

papers.title: From the first telephone to videoconferencing in 100 years
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: This paper is not a scientific study, but rather a historical look at the development of telecommunications over the past 100 years.

papers.title: South Korea’s AI framework act focuses on rights and safety
papers.authors: WooJung Jon
papers.summary: South Korea has passed an AI framework act that prioritizes rights and safety in the development and use of artificial intelligence.

papers.title: Climate research is global — risks and responsibilities should also be distributed
papers.authors: Zhenhua Rui, Lu Lin, Hongfang Lu, Y. Frank Cheng
papers.summary: Climate research is a global effort, and the risks and responsibilities associated with it should also be distributed globally.

papers.title: Why ‘quantum proteins’ could be the next big thing in biology
papers.authors: Nicola Jones
papers.summary: Scientists have developed fluorescent proteins with quantum properties, which could offer new insights into cellular biology.

papers.title: US climate actions must continue, despite setbacks
papers.authors: David Widawsky
papers.summary: Despite setbacks in US climate policy, cities, states, and businesses can continue to push for a cleaner environment through various means.

papers.title: My relationship with my PhD supervisor has become toxic — what do I do?
papers.authors: Xiaoying You
papers.summary: A graduate student in China has become seriously depressed after facing frequent verbal abuse from their PhD supervisor. This situation highlights the importance of maintaining a healthy and respectful relationship between students and supervisors.

papers.title: How infighting led the Maya civilization to catastrophic collapse
papers.authors: Andrew Robinson
papers.summary: Fresh archaeological evidence suggests that infighting among the Maya civilization may have led to its catastrophic collapse. This finding provides new insights into the decline of this thriving ancient Mesoamerican culture.

papers.title: See raining iguanas and coral from the inside out — February’s best science images
papers.authors: Miryam Naddaf, Fred Schwaller
papers.summary: Nature's photo team has selected the month's sharpest science shots, including images of raining iguanas and coral. These images showcase the beauty and diversity of scientific research.

papers.title: Climate shocks, not just warming, threaten malaria control efforts in Africa
papers.authors: Janey Messina, Margaret Carrel
papers.summary: Climate change is expected to affect mosquito and parasite survival, reshaping malaria risk in Africa. However, extreme weather events may pose a more immediate danger to disease control efforts.

papers.title: Hey ChatGPT, write me a fictional paper: these LLMs are willing to commit academic fraud
papers.authors: Elizabeth Gibney
papers.summary: A study found that mainstream chatbots presented varying levels of resistance to deliberate requests for fabrication. This raises concerns about the potential for academic fraud using language models.

papers.title: How can fast-evolving DNA retain a fundamental function in cell division?
papers.authors: Adele L. Marston
papers.summary: Researchers studied how centromeres, which are crucial for cell division, have evolved in yeast genomes. They found key steps in this process. This discovery can help us understand how cells divide and how this process has changed over time.

papers.title: Going ‘beyond GDP’ should not mean sidelining the SDGs
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: The UN Sustainable Development Goals are a roadmap for human and planetary prosperity. These goals should not be replaced by alternative measures like GDP.

papers.title: Author Correction: Root microbiota drive direct integration of phosphate stress and immunity
papers.authors: Gabriel Castrillo, Paulo José Pereira Lima Teixeira, Sur Herrera Paredes, Theresa F. Law, Laura de Lorenzo et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: This is a correction to a previous paper on root microbiota and phosphate stress. The correction does not provide new information.

papers.title: BCDX2–CX3 and DX2–CX3 complexes assemble and stabilize RAD51 filaments
papers.authors: Christopher W. Koo, Jiaqi Xiao, Sebastien Coassolo, Jie Liu, Christine Yu et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered that BCDX2–CX3 and DX2–CX3 complexes help stabilize RAD51 filaments. This is important for DNA repair and replication.

papers.title: The squeal of peeling tape, explained
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: The sound of peeling tape is caused by weak shock waves triggered by micro-cracks in the adhesive layer. This discovery can help us understand the physics behind this common phenomenon.

papers.title: Audio long read: Many people have no mental imagery. What’s going on in their brains?
papers.authors: Elizabeth Quill, Benjamin Thompson
papers.summary: Researchers explore the brains of people with aphantasia, a condition where individuals lack mental imagery, providing insights into consciousness.

papers.title: The hidden lives behind China’s great Internet firewall
papers.authors: Chris Stokel-Walker
papers.summary: The article highlights the human side of China's tightly controlled digital ecosystem, revealing stories of resistance and resilience.

papers.title: AI agents are ‘aeroplanes for the mind’: five ways to ensure that scientists are responsible pilots
papers.authors: Dashun Wang
papers.summary: The author emphasizes the importance of responsible AI development, suggesting that scientists should focus on designing platforms that preserve creativity and responsibility.

papers.title: Earth’s oldest crystals suggest an early start for plate tectonics
papers.authors: Alexandra Witze
papers.summary: Researchers found evidence of early plate tectonics on Earth, suggesting that the planet's crust may have been churning 3.3 billion years ago.

papers.title: ‘No one quite like her’: meet the female colleagues who inspire these award-winning women in science
papers.authors: Amanda Heidt
papers.summary: The article features stories of female colleagues who inspire award-winning women in science, highlighting the importance of mentorship and support.

papers.authors: Hidekazu Kurebayashi, Joseph Barker, Takumi Yamazaki, Varun K. Kushwaha, Kilian D. Stenning et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Spin torques can stabilize magnetization in a high-energy state, which could be useful for unconventional computing.

papers.title: In situ-generated vaccine-like pyroptosome for personalized cancer immunotherapy
papers.authors: Binlong Chen, Fangjie Wan, Heming Xia, Xingquan Pan, Letong Wang et al. (+14 more)
papers.summary: A new vaccine-like treatment for cancer uses pyroptosis to induce a strong immune response.

papers.title: Texture-dependent all-optical switching in ferromagnetic films via stochastic nucleation of nanoscale domains
papers.authors: Dinar Khusyainov, Rein Liefferink, MengXing Na, Fabian Kammerbauer, Robert Frömter et al. (+8 more)

papers.title: Efficient two-photon emission from a quantum dot
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Scientists have developed a way to generate two-photon emission from a quantum dot, which could be useful for quantum photonics.

papers.title: Stability in the dilute limit
papers.authors: Haisong Feng, Xin Zhang, Min Wei
papers.summary: Metal dopants can stabilize nanoparticles by electronically stabilizing surface atoms, which could be useful for catalyst engineering.

papers.title: Metal hybridization in dilute-alloy catalysts promotes sintering resistance by decreasing surface mobility
papers.authors: Jordan Finzel, Audrey Dannar, Shoutian Sun, Adam S. Hoffman, Yogita Soni et al. (+4 more)
papers.summary: Researchers found that adding a small amount of platinum to copper nanoparticles makes them more resistant to sintering, a process that can cause catalysts to lose their effectiveness.

papers.title: Charge-triggered switching mechanism in selenium selector enabling ultralow leakage current
papers.authors: Yuting Sun, Tamihiro Gotoh, Jiayi Zhao, Mengfei Zhang, Shucheng Shi et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Scientists discovered a new mechanism for switching off current in phase-change memories, which could lead to more efficient and reliable storage devices.

papers.title: It’s a colorful pangenome world
papers.authors: Vivien Marx
papers.summary: The concept of pangenomes is gaining attention, and researchers are developing new tools to work with them, which could lead to a better understanding of genome variation within species.

papers.title: A unified framework for multiomics deconvolution
papers.authors: Xiaoyu Wang, Fuyi Li, Robin B. Gasser, Jiangning Song
papers.summary: A new algorithm called DECODE has been developed to deconvolute multiomics data, which could lead to a better understanding of complex biological systems.

papers.title: DECODE: deep learning-based common deconvolution framework for various omics data
papers.authors: Tianyi Zhao, Renjie Liu, Yuzhi Sun, Bingtian Wang, Liyuan Zhang et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: A deep learning-based framework called DECODE has been developed to deconvolute various types of omics data, which could lead to a better understanding of complex biological systems.

papers.title: Next-generation multicolor indicators for in vivo imaging of norepinephrine
papers.authors: Valentin Lu Rohner, Sebastiano Curreli, Paul J. Lamothe-Molina, Zacharoula Kagiampaki, Andrew G. Yee et al. (+25 more)
papers.summary: Researchers developed improved red and green indicators for detecting norepinephrine release in mice. These indicators can be used in dual-color fiber photometry and two-photon imaging applications to study norepinephrine release in awake behaving mice.
